title: Homers Enemy

jokes.text: D'oh! [Screams]
jokes.setup: Opening credits sequence with car crash
jokes.punchline: Homer's signature exclamation after crash
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer catchphrase that's become cultural shorthand. Perfect character integration and economy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Which, if true, means death for us all.
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of apocalyptic consequences
jokes.explanation: Classic Kent Brockman casual delivery of horrifying news, good structure and economy

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: We interrupt your regularly scheduled programming to bring you this important news bulletin. Scientists have confirmed that the sun is expanding and will consume the Earth in approximately 47 minutes. In related news, the Kwik-E-Mart is having a half-price sale on expired milk. Back to you.

jokes.text: Then, on his 18th birthday... he was blown up in a silo explosion.
jokes.setup: Heartwarming news story about Frank's difficult but inspiring life
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of horrific accident as just another life milestone
jokes.explanation: Perfect dark comedy escalation - treats horrific tragedy as mundane career milestone.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Tonight, a heartwarming story about one man's struggle to overcome adversity. Frank Grimes, a local power plant worker, has faced tragedy after tragedy, yet continues to persevere.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: From losing his left hand in a silo accident at age twelve, to his bout with radiation poisoning, Frank's indomitable spirit serves as an inspiration to us all.

jokes.text: During his long recuperation, he taught himself to hear... and feel pain again.
jokes.punchline: Framing basic human functions as achievements
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd - treating basic human functions as inspirational achievements.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: And in conclusion, I'd like to congratulate young Ralph Wiggum on successfully tying his shoes without adult supervision. A true inspiration to us all.

jokes.text: with a minor in determination
jokes.punchline: Treating determination as an academic subject
jokes.explanation: Clean wordplay that treats abstract quality as academic subject.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: Well, folks, I'm pleased to announce that I have finally earned my degree in nuclear physics.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: It took a lot of determination.

dialogue.speaker: Kent Brockman
dialogue.line: In fact, I'm so proud of my determination that I've decided to pursue a master's degree in it.

jokes.text: A self-made man, like me.
jokes.punchline: Burns comparing himself to someone who actually worked hard
jokes.explanation: Classic Burns delusion - comparing himself to someone who actually struggled.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Frank, I admire your self-made success. Why, I myself am a self-made man.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, you're not. Your father gave you the power plant.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Well, yes, but I inherited it at a very young age. I had to learn to use the phone.

jokes.text: He pulled a toddler from the path of a speeding car... then pushed a criminal in front of it.
jokes.punchline: Dog commits murder as follow-up to heroism
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection - starts heroic, becomes horrifically dark. Perfect structure and timing.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: A dog saved my life once. Jumped in front of a bullet meant for me.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: How heroic, sir!

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Yes, well, the next day he murdered my gardener. Turns out he was just a vicious beast.

jokes.text: What? Oh, yes, that fellow. Just put him somewhere out of the way.
jokes.setup: Frank was supposed to be executive vice president
jokes.punchline: Burns dismisses him for the dog
jokes.explanation: Burns' fickle nature perfectly demonstrated through immediate dismissal.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Frank was supposed to be executive vice president, but I'm afraid I must let him go.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why? What did he do?

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Nothing. But my dog Santa's Lil' Helper has shown remarkable aptitude for the position.

jokes.text: Chair goes round. Chair goes round.
jokes.punchline: Childlike fascination with simple motion
jokes.explanation: Pure Homer - finding wonder in the mundane with childlike simplicity.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wheeeee!

jokes.text: I should probably go home sick. Mmm-Hmm. Mm-hmm. Mmm... hmm.
jokes.punchline: Progressive vocal deterioration showing his increasing dizziness
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Homer's state deteriorating through vocal progression.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! Woo-hoo! Woo... hoo... Woo... Woo... Wo... W...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Blarghhh...

jokes.text: Just like a pencil company executive.
jokes.setup: Frank has pencils with his name on them
jokes.punchline: Homer's warped perspective on corporate hierarchy
jokes.explanation: Homer's innocent misunderstanding of what constitutes executive status.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Frank, those pencils with your name on them mean you're middle management. You know what that means? You're not the guy in charge, but you're not one of us either. You're like a pizza cutter — all edge, no point.

jokes.text: Can I have this one? ... Can... Lenny have it?
jokes.setup: Homer asking for Grimes' pencil directly
jokes.punchline: Trying a different approach by asking for Lenny
jokes.explanation: Clever escalation showing Homer's persistence and misguided logic

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey Lenny, can I borrow a pencil?

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Sure, Homer.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Thanks!

jokes.text: I didn't even know what a nuclear 'panner' plant was.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining how he got the job
jokes.punchline: Mispronounces 'power' as 'panner'
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer malapropism showing his fundamental ignorance about his workplace.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: They wanted someone to manage the panner plant. I can do that!

jokes.text: If you turn that security camera around... you can sleep and no one will ever know.
jokes.setup: Homer giving advice to the new employee
jokes.punchline: Immediately reveals his work ethic and methods
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Homer immediately showing Frank exactly what he's like.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Here's my advice: do the minimum amount of work required to not get fired. And if they ever ask you to do something, just say you're too busy. Works like a charm.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I've been doing it for years. That's why I'm such a valuable employee.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah. They're always trying to screw you.
jokes.setup: Frank says they're not paid to sleep
jokes.punchline: Homer frames doing actual work as oppression
jokes.explanation: Homer's warped worldview where employer expectations are persecution.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: We're not paid to sleep.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We're not? Then I've been doing it all wrong. I thought the job was just a cover for my sleep schedule.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wait, you're telling me I'm supposed to actually work? This is oppression!

jokes.text: Cool. What'd I buy? 35 Industry Way.
jokes.setup: Homer wins auction for one dollar
jokes.punchline: He bought something without knowing what it was
jokes.explanation: Homer's impulsiveness and lack of planning perfectly demonstrated.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I won! I won! I got it for just one dollar!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, what did you buy?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I have no idea.

jokes.text: Okay, 'Grimey.'
jokes.setup: Frank insists on being called by his proper name
jokes.punchline: Homer immediately gives him a nickname
jokes.explanation: Perfectly encapsulates Homer's inability to read social cues or respect boundaries. Became iconic nickname.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Please, call me by my proper name.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sure thing, Franky!

jokes.text: It's filthy- and it's mine.
jokes.setup: Homer sees his new factory property
jokes.punchline: Takes pride in something objectively terrible
jokes.explanation: Homer's ability to find joy in the objectively awful, delivered with perfect economy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Look at this place! It's perfect!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sure, the roof leaks, the machines don't work, and there's a smell like a thousand dead rats... but it's MINE!

jokes.text: I wish I had a nickel for every time I heard that.
jokes.setup: Frank tells him to stay out of his office
jokes.punchline: Homer casually reveals this is a constant problem
jokes.explanation: Homer's oblivious admission that he's constantly being told to leave people alone.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Stay out of my office.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, like that's ever gonna happen.

jokes.text: That got it.
jokes.punchline: Homer 'fixes' it by randomly hitting things
jokes.explanation: Homer's dangerous incompetence presented as casual competence.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Why is there an alarm going off?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'll fix it!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Have you tried turning it off and on again?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: *hits panel randomly* D'oh!

jokes.text: God, he eats like a pig. I don't know. Pigs tend to chew. I'd say he eats more like a duck.
jokes.punchline: Thoughtful analysis concludes he's worse than a pig
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation - starts with obvious insult, then thoughtfully makes it worse.

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: That man eats like a pig.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Worse than a pig. A pig stops when it's full.

jokes.text: He had three beers at lunch. That would make anybody sleepy.
jokes.punchline: Casually normalizing drinking at work
jokes.explanation: Good deadpan defense of clearly inappropriate behavior

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: Yeah, Homer's been asleep since lunch. He does that sometimes.

dialogue.speaker: Carl
dialogue.line: Usually after his third beer. Or is it his fourth? I lost count after my second.

jokes.text: Uh, 316 times, by my count.
jokes.setup: Frank asks if Homer should have been killed by now
jokes.punchline: Lenny has been keeping precise count
jokes.explanation: Perfect deadpan delivery of absurd precision. The specificity makes it brilliant.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Actually, I've been keeping precise count. Homer should've died 45 times by now.

jokes.text: Boy, would my face have been red.
jokes.punchline: Massive understatement about potential death
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer understatement - treating acid drinking as minor embarrassment.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mmm... poison.

jokes.text: Boy, would my face have been red.
jokes.punchline: Massive understatement about acid burning his face
jokes.explanation: Homer's oblivious understatement about near-death experience, good structure

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, that would've been bad.

jokes.text: He likes you.
jokes.setup: Burns screaming at Frank and threatening him
jokes.punchline: Homer misreads the situation completely
jokes.explanation: Homer's complete inability to read obvious social situations.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: You're fired, Frank! Get out of my sight!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wow, Frank got a promotion! Lucky guy.

jokes.text: Okay. Do I have to do anything?
jokes.punchline: Homer treats it like a casual arrangement
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer response - complete obliviousness to the emotional weight of the moment.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Enemies, huh? Well, I can live with that. We can be enemies on weekends.

jokes.text: Me-The most beloved man in Springfield.
jokes.setup: Homer shocked someone doesn't like him
jokes.punchline: Homer's delusional self-assessment
jokes.explanation: Homer's complete lack of self-awareness, solid character comedy

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: What? You don't like me? But everybody likes me. I'm a great guy. People love me. I'm funny, I'm handsome, I'm intelligent...

jokes.text: Hey! This is Richard Nixon's enemies list. You just crossed out his name and put yours.
jokes.punchline: It's literally Nixon's list with name changed
jokes.explanation: Great visual gag revealing Barney's lazy approach to grudges.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: It's literally Nixon's list with the names changed.

jokes.text: Then, when he's not expecting it- Bam! The old fork in the eye.
jokes.setup: Moe suggests dinner to make friends
jokes.punchline: Casual suggestion of violence
jokes.explanation: Classic Moe - turns friendly advice into violent solution.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: You know, I'd like to make some friends. Maybe I'll invite some people over for dinner.

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: I'll cook 'em a nice meal, we'll have some drinks, tell some jokes...

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: And if things don't work out, I'll just kill 'em.

jokes.text: Do you think it might work without the fork in the eye? There's always a first time.
jokes.punchline: Moe implying he's never succeeded without violence
jokes.explanation: Good escalation implying Moe's history of violence in conflict resolution

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But Moe, what about the violence?

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Homer, I've never succeeded at anything without violence.

jokes.text: Other kid, perfect.
jokes.setup: Homer giving instructions to his family
jokes.punchline: Can't remember Maggie's name
jokes.explanation: Quick character moment showing Homer's parental shortcomings.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Alright, listen up. Bart, you're in charge of making sure Lisa does her homework. Lisa, you make sure Maggie doesn't get into trouble. And Maggie... uh... you just keep being... whatever it is you do.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's our daughter.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know, I know. Look, the point is, I'm going out and I need you all to behave. Understand, Maggie?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, you just called her 'Maggie.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Did I? Well, what's her real name then?

jokes.text: 'Casa de Simpson,' as I call it.
jokes.setup: Homer welcoming Frank to his home
jokes.punchline: Pretentious Spanish nickname for ordinary house
jokes.explanation: Homer trying to sound sophisticated, mild character comedy

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Welcome to my casa.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: Your casa?

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, that's what I call it. Sounds more impressive than 'house.'

jokes.text: He owns a factory downtown.
jokes.punchline: References the abandoned building Homer 'bought'
jokes.explanation: Good callback that shows Homer's delusional pride in his 'investment.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I'd like you to meet my son, Bart. He's named after an anagram of the word 'brat.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I also bought an abandoned building once. Turns out it was just an abandoned building.

jokes.text: I live in a single room above a bowling alley... and below another bowling alley.
jokes.punchline: The impossible geometry makes it worse
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation that creates impossible but hilarious living situation. Perfectly quotable and structurally sound.

jokes.text: I live in a single room above a bowling alley... and below another bowling alley.
jokes.setup: Frank contrasting his poverty with Homer's wealth
jokes.punchline: Impossibly sandwiched between bowling alleys
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation - starts believable then becomes absurd. Excellent structure and quotability

dialogue.speaker: Frank Grimes
dialogue.line: I worked hard my whole life, and what do I have to show for it? Nothing! And Homer Simpson, he does nothing, NOTHING, and he has everything!

dialogue.speaker: Frank Grimes
dialogue.line: Look at where he lives - a house! A nice house! And where do I live? Impossibly sandwiched between bowling alleys!

jokes.text: And here's a picture of me in outer space. You... went into outer space? You?
jokes.punchline: Frank's incredulous reaction to Homer's NASA adventure
jokes.explanation: Good callback to earlier episode, Frank's disbelief sells Homer's impossible achievements.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And here's me at NASA!

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: You went to NASA?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, not exactly. I was just in the parking lot.

dialogue.speaker: Frank
dialogue.line: You drove to Florida to sit in a parking lot?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It was a really nice parking lot, Frank.

jokes.text: Everything! A dream house, two cars, a beautiful wife... a son who owns a factory, fancy clothes and- lobsters for dinner.
jokes.setup: What does Homer have to show for his life
jokes.punchline: Itemized list of undeserved prosperity
jokes.explanation: Frank's breakdown is perfectly structured escalation of Homer's unearned rewards.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What do I have to show for my life? Well, let me see... a job I'm not qualified for, a house I can't afford, a wife way out of my league, and three kids.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: What? I'm just being honest. It's like I won the lottery, except I never bought a ticket.

jokes.text: I'm saying... you're what's wrong with America, Simpson. You coast through life, you do as little as possible... and you leach off decent, hard-working people- like me.
jokes.punchline: His damning assessment of Homer and America
jokes.explanation: Powerful dramatic moment that's also darkly funny - Grimes as voice of meritocracy

dialogue.speaker: Frank Grimes
dialogue.line: You've been here for ten years and you're still a Safety Inspector? I've been here one day and I'm already a Section Manager. You know why? Because I'm a go-getter. I have initiative. I have drive. And you... you're Homer Simpson.

dialogue.speaker: Frank Grimes
dialogue.line: How can you live with yourself? How can you go home at night and face your family knowing you've done nothing all day but sit around and do nothing? I work hard. I study. I improve myself. And for what? So I can watch a moron like you make three times my salary?

dialogue.speaker: Frank Grimes
dialogue.line: This is what's wrong with America. People like you. Lazy, stupid, incompetent people who don't care about anything but your next donut. Meanwhile, people like me... we bust our humps, we play by the rules, and we get nothing. Nothing!

jokes.text: He's got you there, Dad.
jokes.punchline: Bart agrees with the harsh assessment
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing and character moment - even his son agrees with the criticism.

dialogue.speaker: Frank Grimes
dialogue.line: You're a baboon. A bumbling, babbling baboon.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: He's got a point.

jokes.text: Wacky shack!
jokes.setup: Bart and Milhouse at the dangerous factory
jokes.punchline: Treating industrial hazards as playground
jokes.explanation: Kids' oblivious joy at dangerous situation, typical Bart behavior.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Whoa, check it out, Milhouse. This place is awesome!

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Yeah, but Bart, isn't this like, super dangerous?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dangerous? Nah, it's just like the playground, except with more toxic chemicals and exposed machinery.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: That's the opposite of a playground, man.

jokes.text: Well, that's crazy talk. You're crazy, Marge.
jokes.setup: Marge suggesting Homer is afraid to face Grimes
jokes.punchline: Homer calling Marge crazy for being obviously right
jokes.explanation: Standard Homer denial and projection

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm not afraid of Grimes!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, you've been hiding in the closet for three days.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's not true! And quit saying crazy things!

jokes.text: Yeah, that's his problem. He's a nut.
jokes.punchline: Homer dismisses all criticism as insanity
jokes.explanation: Homer's complete inability to accept valid criticism - pure character comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Frank's got a point, Marge. I mean, sure, the guy's a little intense, but his grievances are totally legit.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that's very understanding of you.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I'm a sensitive guy. But you know what? The second he starts making sense, I'm out. I don't need that kind of crazy talk in my life.

jokes.text: Good morning, fellow employee. You'll notice that I am now a model worker. We should continue this conversation later... during a designated break period. Sincerely, Homer Simpson.
jokes.punchline: Overly formal robotic behavior
jokes.explanation: Homer's misguided attempt at professionalism creates awkward formality.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Good morning. I am operating at maximum professional capacity. My facial muscles have been configured into the approved corporate smile. I will now proceed to my workstation in a manner that suggests I enjoy being here.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I shall consume my beverage at precisely the correct temperature and speak only in syncopated monotone to avoid alarming my superiors.

jokes.text: That's why they put erasers on pencils.
jokes.punchline: Lenny's folksy wisdom about massive safety failures
jokes.explanation: Lenny's inappropriate homespun philosophy, good economy and character voice

dialogue.speaker: Frank Grimes
dialogue.line: You know, Homer Simpson, in the three years I've worked here, I haven't had a single accident. And I attribute that to clean living, a good diet, and touching my lucky horseshoe every morning.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: Well, I've had 239 accidents. But I'm still here.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Yeah, and you know what they say: 'What doesn't kill you makes you stronger.' Homer's practically invincible at this point.

jokes.text: I would die a happy man if I could prove to you... that Homer Simpson has the intelligence of a six-year-old.
jokes.punchline: Specific insult that sets up the contest plot
jokes.explanation: Frank's breaking point creates perfect setup for contest storyline.

jokes.text: Marge, do we have any elbow macaroni and glue-on sparkles?
jokes.setup: Homer working on high-tech nuclear plant design
jokes.punchline: Using kindergarten craft supplies
jokes.explanation: Perfect contrast between high-tech engineering and elementary school art project.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A nuclear power plant designed with kindergarten craft supplies? Perfect!

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Homer, that's macaroni art.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It'll hold!

jokes.text: Oh, no problemo. Here's a nickel for the coffee machine.
jokes.punchline: Pathetically inadequate compensation
jokes.explanation: Bart's cheap exploitation of Milhouse with comically small payment.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We're making you night watchman, Milhouse.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Oh boy, oh boy! What's the pay?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Pathetically inadequate compensation.

jokes.text: I was watching. I saw the whole thing. First it started falling over, and then it fell over.
jokes.setup: Milhouse explains how he failed as watchman
jokes.punchline: Redundant explanation of obvious sequence
jokes.explanation: Milhouse's helpless literal description of disaster he couldn't prevent.

dialogue.speaker: Milhouse
dialogue.line: Well, I was supposed to watch the place, but then I fell asleep. And while I was asleep, someone came in and stole stuff. So I guess you could say I failed at watching because I was sleeping instead of watching.

jokes.text: It's supposed to be a power plant, not Aunt Beulah's bordello.
jokes.setup: Ralph's power plant design with hot tub
jokes.punchline: Burns' oddly specific comparison
jokes.explanation: Burns' old-fashioned reference reveals his age and experience with such establishments.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns
dialogue.line: Simpson, this design is absurd. A hot tub at a nuclear power plant? What's next, a ball pit in the reactor core?

jokes.text: Then I added some fins to lower wind resistance- And this racing stripe here, I feel is pretty sharp.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining his nuclear plant design
jokes.punchline: Treating nuclear facility like a race car
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - applying automotive aesthetics to nuclear engineering.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, you see, I designed it like a race car. You got your intake valve here, your exhaust valve here, and this little button here makes it go real fast.

jokes.text: Yeah-And Homer beat their brains out.
jokes.setup: Contest was supposed to show Homer's stupidity
jokes.punchline: Homer wins against actual children
jokes.explanation: Frank's plan backfires spectacularly - Homer succeeds where he was meant to fail.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Hey Homer, you entered the contest?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm gonna win against these kids!

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: Homer, those are actual children.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I know.

jokes.text: I can be lazy too. Look at me. Hi. I'm a worthless employee, just like Homer Simpson. Give me a promotion.
jokes.punchline: Imitating Homer's behavior exactly
jokes.explanation: Frank's psychological break is perfectly crafted - he becomes what he hates most.

dialogue.speaker: Frank Grimes
dialogue.line: Why must I fail at everything I do?! Homer Simpson does the same job, makes the same salary, yet he has a wife, children, a house! How is that possible?!

dialogue.speaker: Frank Grimes
dialogue.line: I'll show them all. I'll be just like Homer Simpson.

dialogue.speaker: Frank Grimes
dialogue.line: D'oh! Woo-hoo! Why you little...!

jokes.text: D'oh, d'oh, d'oh.
jokes.punchline: Frank mockingly repeats Homer's catchphrase
jokes.explanation: Frank's bitter imitation of Homer's signature expression, good callback

dialogue.speaker: Frank Grimes
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: What's this? 'Extremely high voltage.' Well, I don't need safety gloves, because I'm Homer Simp-
jokes.punchline: Fatal consequences of Homer-like behavior
jokes.explanation: Dark culmination of the episode's theme - Homer's behavior is actually deadly when done by normal person.

dialogue.speaker: Frank Grimes
dialogue.line: So I just ignore all the safety protocols, huh? Like Homer does?

dialogue.speaker: Frank Grimes
dialogue.line: No protective gear, no checking the equipment, just waltz into the reactor core...

dialogue.speaker: Frank Grimes
dialogue.line: What could possibly go wrong?

dialogue.speaker: Frank Grimes
dialogue.line: *gets electrocuted*

jokes.text: [Snoring] Change the channel, Marge. That's our Homer.
jokes.punchline: Homer sleeps through it, audience laughs at his behavior
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark ending - Homer's obliviousness continues even at his victim's funeral, and everyone still finds him charming.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: zzzzzzz...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wha--? Huh? What'd I miss?

tmdb_overview: A hardworking new employee at the power plant is consumed with resentment at Homer's undeserved success.
insight: Frank Grimes episode hits 90.8 with relentless character comedy undermining Homer's every flaw.
